changeReason: Improved translation from Japanese. Old: "Inquiry Request"

TextENGNew: Okinomiya Police Station — Command Center Transmission Log

changeReason: Old uses '8:08 PM' (12-hour format); new uses '20:08' (24-hour/military format). The JPN uses ２０時 (20-hour notation). For a police command center log, 24-hour format is more appropriate and matches the original.

TextENGNew: "Okinomiya PS here, reading you loud and clear."
"Ah, could you run a plate check for us? Shishibone, Ha 43-44."
changeReason: Old translates 車両ナンバー照会 as 'look up a license plate'; new says 'run a plate check.' Additionally, old includes markup tags '@bHa.@<は@>' while new uses plain 'Ha' — the new version removes the in-line formatting tags from the translated text.

TextENGNew: "Confirmed: Shishibone, Ha 43-44. Could you hold a moment while we look it up?"
"Please."
changeReason: Old translates お願いします as 'Thank you'; new says 'Please.' The JPN literally means 'please (do it)' — a request, not an expression of thanks.

TextENGNew: Plate Search Results
Shishibone, Ha 43-44
Owner Code: 6034344
changeReason: Old redacts the owner code; new provides the actual data matching the Japanese.

TextENGNew: <Ordinary citizen, resident of Hinamizawa Village, Shishibone City. No prior record.>
changeReason: Old translates as an address with redactions; new provides the actual content: 'Ordinary citizen, no prior record.'

TextENGNew: Vehicle Type: Station wagon
changeReason: Old redacts vehicle type; new provides 'Station wagon' matching the Japanese.

TextENGNew: Theft Report: None
Special Notes: Nothing of note
``Okinomiya PS to Ooishi. We have the plate results from earlier. ...Ooishi, do you read? ...Ooishi car, please respond.``
changeReason: Old says 'Okinomiya Security Police'; new says 'Okinomiya PS.' The JPN 興宮ＰＳ uses the abbreviation PS (Police Station). 'Security Police' is a mistranslation of ＰＳ in this context.

TextENGNew: "...Hmm? ...Bad signal, maybe... Ooishi car, please resp—"
"No answer."
"Ooishi-san ran a plate check? Whose car was it?"
"Belongs to a villager. ...Utterly unremarkable."
``...So who is it? If Ooishi-san was asking, there must be something to it, right?``
changeReason: Old says 'it can't be anyone good, right?'; new says 'there must be something to it, right?' The JPN 只者じゃないんだろ means 'they're no ordinary person, right?' — implying the person is notable/suspicious, not necessarily 'not good.'

TextENGNew: "The notes column is completely empty. No S-group flag either. Not even any demerit points."
``...Ha ha ha. Maybe someone flashed their lights at him and he took offence? That man's the type to hold a grudge forever, you know.``
changeReason: Old says 'Maybe it passed him on the road and he got mad'; new says 'Maybe someone flashed their lights at him and he took offence.' The JPN パッシング refers to flashing headlights (from English 'passing'), not being overtaken on the road.
